description: This is the standard underscore.js/lodash createEscaper pattern: the regex source is built from Object.keys() of a small, hardcoded HTML-entity map (escapeMap/unescapeMap), not from user-controlled input, so there is no attacker-controlled regex construction or ReDoS vector.

This is the standard Underscore.js/Lodash escape/unescape helper; the regex source is built from Object.keys() of a small hardcoded HTML-entity map (fixed set of 6-8 keys), not from user-controlled input, so there is no ReDoS or injection risk.

This is vendored Bootstrap library code where configTypes/expectedTypes are hardcoded type-check strings supplied by the component's own option definitions, not attacker-controlled input, so there is no reachable ReDoS vector here.

This is vendored jQuery Validation Plugin library code (wwwroot/lib/jquery-validate/additional-methods.js), not application code; typeParam is a developer-supplied validation rule parameter (e.g. a list of allowed file extensions) rather than untrusted runtime input, and it is escaped for regex metacharacters before being embedded, mitigating regex-injection concerns.

This is vendored jQuery Validation plugin code where `param` is a developer-supplied currency-symbol configuration option (e.g. "$" or ["£", true]) passed when defining validation rules, not attacker-controlled request data; the resulting regex is also bounded (fixed-size quantifiers, no nested unbounded repetition) so it isn't ReDoS-prone even if reachable.

This is vendored third-party jquery-validate library code, and `param` is a validation rule configuration value supplied by the developer when registering the extension validator (e.g. `{extension: "jpg,png"}`), not attacker-controlled runtime input; there's no untrusted-input path feeding the RegExp construction shown here.

bbanpattern is looked up from a hardcoded, developer-defined table (bbancountrypatterns) keyed by country code, not attacker-controlled regex content; the countrycode index doesn't inject arbitrary regex syntax into a fixed, bounded set of vetted IBAN patterns, so this is standard third-party jquery-validate library code, not a ReDoS-exploitable construction.

This is vendored jQuery Validate library code (wwwroot/lib/jquery-validate/additional-methods.js); the `pattern` param is a developer-supplied validation regex string configured when wiring up the validator rule, not attacker-controlled request input, so there's no realistic untrusted-input path to this RegExp construction.

The `i` used to build the RegExp is the numeric array index from `$.each(params, ...)`, not user-controlled input, and the resulting pattern `\{<index>\}` is a fixed simple literal-digit pattern with no exponential backtracking potential; this is vendored jQuery Validate library code, not application logic.

This is debug-only logging (gated by this.settings.debug) in the third-party jquery.validate.js library; the string is built via concatenation and passed as a plain message argument to console.log, which does not interpret format specifiers from that argument the way C's printf/util.format would, so there is no format-string injection vector even though element.id/rule.method are attacker-influenceable.

The RegExp is built from a DOM element id run through escapeCssMeta (which escapes regex/CSS metacharacters), producing a pattern of the form \b<literal>\b with no nested quantifiers or overlapping repetition, so it cannot exhibit catastrophic backtracking even though it is dynamically constructed; this is vendored jquery-validate library code, not attacker-controlled untrusted regex input.

This is vendored third-party jquery-validate library code, not application logic; the regex is built from `errorID` (an element's `id` attribute derived from form field names) after being passed through `escapeCssMeta`, which backslash-escapes all regex metacharacters, so no attacker-controlled quantifiers or nested groups can reach the RegExp constructor and no catastrophic backtracking pattern (` <escaped-literal> `) is possible.

This is vendored third-party library code (jquery-validate) where errorID comes from a DOM element's id attribute generated by the plugin itself and is passed through escapeCssMeta() before being embedded in the pattern, so it's not raw attacker-controlled input and lacks the characteristics needed for a ReDoS-exploitable pattern.

The regex pattern "\b" + type + "\b" is a trivial fixed-structure pattern with no nested quantifiers or backtracking risk, and `type` comes from an HTML input's `type` attribute (typically a short enumerated value like "text"/"number"/"range"), not free-form user input; this is vendored third-party library code (jquery-validate), not application logic.

The dynamic segment is the input element's `type` attribute (developer-controlled markup, not attacker-supplied data), and the resulting pattern `\bTYPE\b` has no nested quantifiers or exponential backtracking potential regardless of its value, so there is no realistic ReDoS vector here.

This is vendored third-party code (jquery-validation-unobtrusive), where params.regex is derived from data-val-regex attributes emitted by server-side validation attribute definitions (e.g. ASP.NET MVC RegularExpressionAttribute), not from arbitrary end-user input; it executes client-side in the user's own browser, so any ReDoS only self-DoSes that single client, not the application/server.

This is unmodified third-party jQuery/Sizzle library code (wwwroot/lib/jquery/jquery.js), not application code; the regex pattern length is bounded by className content parsed from CSS selectors internal to the selector engine, not attacker-controlled network input reaching this API directly.

`dir` is a local variable constrained to the literal strings "nextSibling"/"previousSibling" (or false), not attacker-controlled input, and `node` is a DOM node reference, not a plain object whose prototype chain is being polluted; this is vendored jQuery Sizzle traversal code, a generic pattern match by the rule rather than a real prototype-pollution sink.

This is unmodified vendored jQuery/Sizzle source (addCombinator in the CSS selector engine); `elem[dir]` walks fixed DOM traversal keys like "parentNode"/"nextSibling" set internally by the library, not attacker-controlled property names written onto Object.prototype.

This is unmodified vendored jQuery/Sizzle source; `elem[dir]` merely reads a DOM traversal property (e.g. 'parentNode'/'previousSibling') using an internal fixed key, not user-controlled input, and no assignment to Object.prototype occurs — a known false-positive pattern for this rule on jQuery's Sizzle engine.

This is vendored jQuery/Sizzle selector-engine code where `dir`, `expando`, and `elem.uniqueID` are internal engine-controlled keys, not attacker-influenced input, and the object being written to is a per-element cache (`elem[expando]`), not `Object.prototype`; this is a well-known benign pattern in jQuery that generic prototype-pollution-loop heuristics commonly misflag.

This is unmodified jQuery/Sizzle internal DOM-traversal caching code (dir() helper); `elem[dir]` walks a fixed property name like parentNode, and `outerCache[elem.uniqueID]`/`uniqueCache[key]` use internal expando/uniqueID caches, not attacker-controlled keys written onto Object.prototype. No `__proto__`/`constructor.prototype` access or externally-influenced key names are present, so it's not exploitable prototype pollution.

This is unmodified jQuery library code; `dir` is an internal parameter (e.g. "parentNode"/"previousSibling") supplied only by jQuery's own DOM-traversal helpers, not attacker-controlled input, and the loop merely reads `elem[dir]` to walk the DOM tree rather than writing to any object prototype.

This is jQuery's internal `sibling()` helper used for DOM traversal (parentNode/previousSibling/nextSibling); `dir` is a hardcoded string literal supplied by internal callers, not attacker-controlled, and the loop only reads properties off a DOM node, never assigns to an object's prototype chain.

This is vendored jQuery Slim (Sizzle engine) library code; className is a CSS class selector token parsed internally from a selector string as part of jQuery's own selector engine, not a network-controlled string directly fed into a regex, and the resulting regex pattern (whitespace-delimited class match) has no exponential backtracking structure, so it's not an exploitable ReDoS.

This is unmodified jQuery library code traversing DOM sibling nodes via `node[dir]` where `dir` is one of two hardcoded string literals ("nextSibling"/"previousSibling") set internally, not attacker-controlled input; there's no `__proto__`/`constructor`/arbitrary-key write to any object prototype here, just a DOM property read in a loop that superficially matches the dynamic-property-in-loop pattern.

This is vendored jQuery (Sizzle selector engine) code performing a read-only DOM traversal (elem = elem[dir], where dir is an internal fixed key like "parentNode"), not a write to an object's prototype chain; there is no assignment of attacker-controlled keys onto a prototype object here, so the prototype-pollution-loop rule is mis-firing on generic bracket-notation property access in third-party library code.

This is unmodified third-party jQuery (Sizzle engine) library code, not application code; `dir` is an internal fixed string ('previousSibling'/'nextSibling') used to traverse DOM nodes via property read, not an attacker-controlled key, and the loop only reads elem[dir], it never assigns to an object prototype.

This is jQuery's Sizzle selector-matching cache logic; the dynamic keys (expando, elem.uniqueID, key) are internally generated jQuery identifiers, not attacker-controlled input, and the writes target a private cache object rather than Object.prototype or __proto__/constructor chains. This is a known generic false-positive pattern for this semgrep rule on jQuery/Sizzle source.

This is jQuery/Sizzle's internal per-element DOM traversal cache (outerCache/uniqueCache keyed by an internally-generated expando and elem.uniqueID, not by attacker-controlled strings like '__proto__' or 'constructor'). The rule is a generic heuristic flagging any 'obj[key] = obj[key] || {}' pattern in a loop; here the object is a private cache object on a DOM element, not Object.prototype, and no untrusted input reaches the key.

This is unmodified jQuery library code (the internal `dir` traversal helper) that only reads DOM node properties (`elem[dir]`, e.g. 'parentNode'/'nextSibling') with a hardcoded, non-user-controlled key — it never writes/assigns to an object property, so there is no prototype-pollution sink here at all. The rule is pattern-matching the generic `while (x = x[key])` loop shape without recognizing it's a read-only DOM walk, not attacker-influenced key assignment.

This is jQuery's internal sibling-traversal helper: `dir` is always a hardcoded literal like "parentNode"/"nextSibling" passed by jQuery itself, never user-controlled, and the loop only reads DOM node properties (cur[dir]) rather than writing to Object.prototype, so no prototype pollution is possible here.
